Reply: Use strong passwords with a mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols.
Avoid simple passwords like 123456 or your name. Use a password manager instead.
Phishing is when attackers trick you into revealing personal information through fake emails or websites.
Always verify the sender's email address and avoid clicking suspicious links.
Malware is malicious software designed to harm or exploit your system.
A virus is a type of malware that spreads by attaching itself to files.
Install trusted antivirus software and keep it updated to protect your system.
A VPN encrypts your internet connection and protects your privacy online.
A firewall monitors incoming and outgoing network traffic and blocks threats.
Hackers are individuals who exploit system vulnerabilities. Ethical hackers help improve security.
Encryption converts data into a secure format that only authorized users can read.
A data breach occurs when sensitive information is accessed without authorization.
Two-factor authentication adds an extra layer of security beyond your password.
2FA requires something you know (password) and something you have (phone/code).
Social engineering tricks people into giving away confidential information.
Spam emails are unwanted messages, often used to spread malware or scams.
Ransomware locks your files and demands payment to restore access.
Always back up your data regularly to avoid data loss from attacks.
Use strong authentication and encryption when storing data in the cloud.
Avoid accessing sensitive information on public Wi-Fi without a VPN.
Identity theft happens when someone uses your personal data fraudulently.
Keep your system and software updated to fix security vulnerabilities.
Security patches fix bugs and vulnerabilities in software.
Authentication verifies your identity before granting access.
Authorization determines what actions you are allowed to perform.
A cyber attack is an attempt to damage or gain unauthorized access to systems.
Brute force attacks try many password combinations until one works.
A DDoS attack floods a system with traffic to make it unavailable.
A botnet is a network of infected devices controlled by attackers.
A keylogger records your keystrokes to steal sensitive information.
Spyware secretly monitors your activities without your consent.
A Trojan disguises itself as legitimate software to trick users.
A worm spreads automatically across networks without user interaction.
Look for HTTPS and a padlock icon before entering sensitive data.
HTTPS encrypts communication between your browser and websites.
Cookies store information about your browsing session.
Protect your privacy by limiting the personal information you share online.
Cybersecurity protects systems, networks, and data from digital attacks.
A threat is any potential danger that can exploit a vulnerability.
A vulnerability is a weakness that can be exploited by attackers.
Risk is the potential for loss or damage when a threat exploits a vulnerability.
Pen testing simulates attacks to find system weaknesses.
Ethical hackers test systems to improve security.
A security policy defines rules and practices to protect systems.
Access control restricts who can access resources.
Biometrics use fingerprints or facial recognition for authentication.
A zero-day exploit targets vulnerabilities before they are fixed.
An insider threat comes from someone within the organization.
Monitoring logs helps detect suspicious activities.
Incident response is how organizations handle security breaches.
Digital signatures verify the authenticity of digital messages.
Security certificates confirm a website's identity.
The dark web is a hidden part of the internet often used anonymously.
Cybercrime involves illegal activities conducted online.
Avoid sharing personal details and use strong passwords to stay safe online.
